focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

Silas cut through Patent Row while the hill still looked hungover from its own miracle. The blessing bills Damaris's boys had pasted for Bellglass Bridge hung in wet curls, and every slick wall along the hill held some leftover quarrel of color: pale gold in one seam, kitchen blue under it, a feral pink climbing the mortar where somebody had shouted bridelight just to see if the city would blush again. People were still stopping in the street to test words against the moss. A teamster in a cracked hat called one patch ferry-veil and got a stripe of green so cold it almost looked silver. Two laundresses laughed, tried widow's lantern, and turned the same stripe blue as skimmed bruises.

He kept walking.

He had slept a little at Ferrymouth, face-down on Junie's spare mattress with the ferry bells punching holes through what was left of the night. The chorus from Bellglass Bridge still ran in his skull, and not the sound of it only, though there had been enough sound to shake rust from iron. He kept feeling a whole city answer at once. He kept feeling what it had been to stand inside that answer and not have to own it. He did not know yet if that felt like relief or bereavement. His mouth tasted of old paste and lamp smoke and the bitter heel of envy. There were still parts of him cheap enough to miss the stage.

Damaris had sent for him at dawn by a runner with one suspender hanging and yesterday's ink on his ear. Gideon had sent for him too, by card, thick cream stock, Verdancy seal pressed in green wax. The card requested a private discussion at his immediate convenience.

That alone told Silas what sort of morning it was.

Damaris's pressroom stood open to the alley for air and got none worth having. The place smelled of hot iron, wet paper, stale chicor...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ay in his knees.

Junie dipped the spoon, blew across it, tasted, and frowned at the fuel's weak burn as if she could insult it back into usefulness.

"Well?" she said.

He could have told her he had kept up. He could have said they did not throw him in the cut. He could even, in a cheaper mood, have offered the board like a triumphant reveal, as if he were saying, Watch me matter again. Watch me come back useful through design. The shape of that performance was still in him. He knew all its joints.

Instead he went to the pump, washed his hands as clean as they would go, and came back for a bit of charcoal from Otis's copy slate.

On the plank's least-rotted face he did not write a name.

He marked out spaces.

The spaces were not elegant. He left room for a broad hand at the top, smaller strips down the sides, a patch left blank where paper could be pasted and scraped away, and another where chalk could take quick changes from stoop to ferry to kitchen. He measured with his thumb, not for beauty but for legibility at a run. He left margins big enough for arguments. He left the center unclaimed.

Otis crept closer, pencil forgotten. "What's it for?"

Silas kept working. The charcoal dragged over old paint ghosts and snagged on damp grain.

"For ferry changes, stove trouble, wake houses, busted stairs, whatever has to reach the next pair of hands before dark," he answered.

Junie watched him a long moment. The yard was full of evening sounds, pans cooling, gulls fighting over offal, a ferry rope slapping wood somewhere out in the dark. At last she nodded toward the board.

"Then make it stout," she said. "The city won't use it gently."

He looked at the empty middle, at the black ribs of space he had left for other hands, and for once did not imagine signing the corner.
